nativeRange.habitat: Wet, open habitats across East Asia: lake and pond margins, marshes, ditches, and other consistently moist or shallowly inundated sites. Grows both fully submersed and emersed.

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) or root tabs in inert gravel
open midground planting space for dense stem clusters
medium-to-high PAR lighting to develop pink-red coloration
iron-rich water-column dosing to push pink/red pigments
stable parameters — sensitive to drastic swings

summary: An East Asian stem plant prized for distinctive paired oval leaves that flush from green through golden brown to soft pink-red under high light, CO2, and iron supplementation. Slower-growing and slightly more demanding than the closely-related Ludwigia repens.
careNotes: Ludwigia ovalis is an Asian stem plant best known for its small, paired oval leaves and a softer pink-to-rose-red coloration than the deeper crimson of L. repens or L. palustris 'Super Red'. Care is consistently rated easy-to-moderate across hobby sources (Modern Aquarium 'easy to care for', Buce Plant 'moderate', Fish Laboratory 'easy for beginners but fragile'); placed here at intermediate because its color expression depends on higher light plus iron dosing and the plant is widely noted as fragile and sensitive to parameter swings. Temperature tolerance is broad: Modern Aquarium cites 70-78 °F (21-26 °C), while Fish Laboratory gives a wider 64-82 °F (18-28 °C) — averaged here to roughly 18-28 °C. pH preference is on the acidic-to-neutral side: Buce Plant 6.0-7.0, Fish Laboratory 5.0-7.0 — taken here as 5.0-7.0. Hardness data is not reliably reported across sources and is left null; the plant tolerates a range typical of soft-to-moderate aquasoil systems. Lighting is the main color lever: medium light is the minimum, but pink/red leaf coloration only emerges reliably under medium-to-high PAR with iron-rich fertilization (Modern Aquarium, Buce Plant, Fish Laboratory all converge here). CO2 is consistently described as optional rather than required, though injection accelerates growth and intensifies color. Growth rate is generally rated fast (Buce Plant, Fish Laboratory — '~4 inches per month'), but on a per-stem basis L. ovalis is widely reported as noticeably slower-growing than L. repens (fw-plant-036); the 'fast' label here reflects source consensus while noting the comparative-slower-than-L.-repens caveat from the assignment brief. Adult submerged height varies with light and CO2: Buce Plant cites 4-8 inches (10-20 cm), Fish Laboratory 10-20 inches (25-50 cm) — combined to 10-50 cm, with most home tanks falling at the lower end. Placement is midground (smaller stems) to background (taller, well-fed stems); the brief specifies midground for the typical home setup. Use nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) for best color and density; root tabs help if planted in inert gravel. Plant several stems in tight groups for visual impact. Avoid drastic parameter changes — Buce Plant and Fish Laboratory both warn the plant is fragile and prone to melt under unstable conditions.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: snip the top of a healthy stem just above a node, remove the lowest leaves, and replant the cutting directly into the substrate. Lateral side shoots emerge from nodes on the parent stem after topping and can be trimmed and replanted in turn. Floating cuttings will root in the water column before planting. Handle gently — stems are fragile and bruised tissue can rot.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Confirms scientific name, family Onagraceae, Asian origin, pH 6.0-7.0, medium light, CO2 not necessary (beneficial), fast growth, cuttings propagation, height 4-8 inches (10-20 cm), moderate care, plant is fragile, iron promotes red/gold/orange coloration, avoid drastic parameter changes (melt risk).

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Confirms scientific name, temperature 70-78 °F (21-26 °C), minimum medium lighting (higher intensity enhances red coloration), midground-to-background placement, propagation via snipping cuttings and replanting, gravel-tolerant, CO2 not mandatory but promotes vigorous growth, iron supplementation recommended for red leaves, easy care, oval paired leaves, green-to-golden-brown-to-red color progression.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Confirms scientific name, Asian origin, temperature 64-82 °F (18-28 °C), pH 5.0-7.0, medium-to-high lighting (~50+ lumens), CO2 optional but beneficial, fast growth (~4 inches/month, up to 10-20 inches tall), background-to-midground placement, stem-cutting propagation, iron supplementation for color (warns about overdosing iron around invertebrates), easy for beginners but fragile, both submersed and emersed growth possible.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ludwigia (plant)
additional.notes: Genus-level reference: confirms family Onagraceae, order Myrtales, genus comprising ~82 aquatic species with cosmopolitan but mainly tropical distribution. No species-level page exists for L. ovalis on Wikipedia (404 on direct URL); used here only for higher-taxon confirmation.
